_meta.description: Country-level phenotypic ciprofloxacin non-susceptibility (CipNS) rates for Salmonella Typhi (NOT NTS). CipNS is defined as intermediate + resistant (MIC ≥0.06–0.125 mg/L depending on CLSI/EUCAST breakpoint used by source), or by presence of QRDR mutations in genomic proxies. Sources are peer-reviewed publications, surveillance networks, and WHO-linked programmes covering 2008–2022.

fields.cipNS_pct: % ciprofloxacin non-susceptible (resistant + intermediate unless noted). null = not available.
fields.cipR_pct: % fully resistant (MIC ≥1 mg/L) where separately reported. null = not available.
fields.N: Number of S. Typhi isolates tested for ciprofloxacin susceptibility. null = not reported.
fields.definition: Susceptibility testing method / breakpoint system used

caveats: Genomic CipNS proxies (QRDR mutations ≥1) from TyphiNET / Global Typhoid Genomics Consortium may differ from phenotypic MIC-based rates.
Hospital-based studies may overestimate resistance relative to community burden.
SEAP (Surveillance for Enteric Fever in Asia Project) uses CLSI disc diffusion; 'FQ non-susceptibility' = combined intermediate + resistant.
South Asian figures approaching 100% likely reflect facility selection bias toward referral hospitals.
African figures are generally lower but vary substantially between sub-regions.
Data should be treated as indicative benchmarks, not definitive national prevalence estimates.

data.definition: CLSI disc diffusion (FQ non-susceptibility = I + R)
data.notes: SEAP Phase II prospective surveillance (Dhaka). CipNS persisted >90% since 2001; reached 98% by 2022 in 24-year surveillance (N=12,435 total).
data.source: Qamar et al. Clin Infect Dis 2020 (SEAP); Islam et al. PLOS NTD 2024

data.notes: 24-year retrospective surveillance at paediatric hospitals and private clinics in Dhaka. CipNS reached 95% by 2001 and remained >90% throughout. In 2022: 98% (1,266/1,298). Representative long-term trend estimate for 2015–2022 is ≥95%.
data.source: Islam et al. PLOS Negl Trop Dis 2024

data.definition: CLSI disc diffusion (FQ non-susceptibility = I + R)
data.notes: SEAP Phase II prospective surveillance (Kathmandu). A separate 2018 single-centre study (Kathmandu, N=46) found 97.8% FQ non-susceptibility (43.5% R + 54.3% I).
data.source: Qamar et al. Clin Infect Dis 2020 (SEAP); Acharya et al. Front Microbiol 2021

data.definition: CLSI disc diffusion (FQ non-susceptibility = I + R)
data.notes: SEAP Phase II prospective surveillance (Karachi). Year-by-year breakdown from national surveillance (Continued Outbreak paper, N=1,803): 2017 ciprofloxacin non-susceptible 93% (N=93), 2018 95% (N=175), 2019 98% (N=1,535). XDR typhoid (ceftriaxone-resistant + ciprofloxacin non-susceptible) reached 57% of all S. Typhi 2017–2019; fully ciprofloxacin-resistant fraction rose from 19% (2017) to 58% (2019).
data.source: Qamar et al. Clin Infect Dis 2020 (SEAP); Klemm et al. mBio 2018; Fatima et al. PMC8045622

data.definition: CLSI MIC; CipNS = MIC ≥0.12 µg/mL; CipR = MIC ≥1 µg/mL
data.notes: Large multicentre surveillance across India (northern and southern). Only 2.1% susceptible. Northern India: 41% fully resistant; Southern India: 29% fully resistant. A post-COVID-19 surveillance study found 99.3% CipNS. One Kolkata tertiary centre (2017–2019) found 0% CipR in 2017, 45.5% in 2018, and 100% from 2019 onwards—reflecting rapid escalation.
data.source: Britto et al. J Infect Dis 2021 (PMC8892543); Mandal et al. Antibiotics 2022

data.definition: CLSI; decreased ciprofloxacin susceptibility (DCS) = MIC ≥0.12 to ≤0.5 µg/mL; full resistance not detected
data.notes: Phnom Penh hospital surveillance. 2008–2012: 100% DCS (N=35); 2013–2015: 93.1% DCS (N=29). gyrA Ser83Phe mutation in 60/62 DCS isolates. No fully resistant isolates detected.
data.source: Bodhidatta et al. PLOS NTD 2017 (PMC5624643)

data.notes: Southern Vietnam has been a fluoroquinolone resistance hotspot since the 1990s. DCS/NARST rates reached 90–98% by early 2000s and remained very high through 2008. More recent data (2015–2022 period) not retrieved with precise N; genomic data from TyphiNET/Global Genomics Consortium indicate continued high CipNS. Indonesia study covering Vietnam isolates (2007–2008, N=51) showed 0% resistance, suggesting northern hospital sampling—likely an outlier relative to endemic southern data.
data.source: Pham Van Minh et al. various; Kariuki et al. AAC 2015 (PMC4249406)

data.definition: CLSI; intermediate = MIC 0.12–0.5 µg/mL; resistant = MIC ≥1 µg/mL
data.notes: 18-year Mahosot Hospital surveillance (Vientiane). Resistant: 12/854 (1.4%); Intermediate: 39/854 (4.6%); Combined CipNS ~6%. Majority of isolates pan-susceptible. Ofloxacin remains first-line treatment. Period 2015–2018 not separately stratified but overall rate is very low.
data.source: Dance et al. Am J Trop Med Hyg 2020 (PMC7124924)

data.notes: Five-year review from Tangerang hospital found low ciprofloxacin resistance (<5%); 2007–2009 multi-country study (N=55 Indonesian isolates) showed 0% resistant. ARSP Philippines 2013–2014 genomic data showed sparse QRDR mutations. Indonesia generally remains low-resistance but surveillance is limited.
data.source: Surjadjaja et al. Southeast Asian J Trop Med 2017 (PMID 28484494); Kariuki et al. AAC 2015

data.notes: ARSP 15-year report (2004–2018): S. Typhi ciprofloxacin resistance remained <10% without significant variation. 2013–2014 genomic surveillance (N=17 S. Typhi) showed sparse QRDR mutations (5/17 with DCS genotype). Exact phenotypic % not published in accessible tables. iNTS ciprofloxacin resistance was 8.7% (2014–2018), but this is NTS not Typhi.
data.source: ARSP 15-year report (Issuu); Argimón et al. Trans R Soc Trop Med Hyg 2022 (PMC9717386)

data.definition: Disc diffusion; EUCAST/CLSI breakpoints; reduced susceptibility = MIC ≥0.125 µg/mL
data.notes: Nairobi (Mukuru informal settlement) study reported 43% reduced susceptibility in contemporary S. Typhi. An earlier Kenya Medical Research Institute multi-site study found 20% (2012) rising to 43% (2013). A 2004–2007 retrospective study found 69% (100/144) reduced susceptibility in Nairobi isolates. Trend is upward. Multicountry sub-Saharan Africa molecular analysis (PMC4772832) identified Kenya as having the highest DCS rates in the region.
data.source: Omulo et al. BMC Microbiol 2018 (PMC); Okoko et al. PLOS ONE 2024 (PMC10889635)

data.definition: EUCAST MIC; low-level resistance = MIC 0.125–0.25 µg/mL
data.notes: Predominance of MDR genotype 4.3.1.1 (H58) with low-level ciprofloxacin resistance via gyrA D87G mutation. 43/62 (69%) had low-level CipNS; no full resistance detected. Pilot study 2012–2013 showed similar pattern.
data.source: Deen et al. PLOS NTD 2024 (PMC; doi:10.1371/journal.pntd.0012132)

data.notes: National reference laboratory isolates. CipR rose from 0% (2012) to 4.2% (2014) to 22% (2017). During the 2018 Harare outbreak, ciprofloxacin resistance was 33% among hospitalised patients and reached 75% in Kuwadzana suburb. The H58 genotype (93.2% of isolates) carries qnrS on IncN plasmid. CipNS ~= CipR in this dataset (no separate intermediate category reported).
data.source: Munyenyiwa et al. BMC Infect Dis 2019 (PMC6544939); Tickell et al. Lancet Microbe 2023

data.definition: QRDR mutation genotyping (genomic proxy); phenotypic: 29 DCS + 1 fully resistant / 502 isolates = 6%
data.notes: Blantyre (Queen Elizabeth Central Hospital). Oct 2016–Aug 2019: 4/177 (2%) QRDR mutations. Sept–Oct 2019: 6/18 (33%) spike coinciding with increased ciprofloxacin prescribing. Broader hospital screening (2019–2022, N=502): 30/502 (6%) with DCS or full resistance. Dramatic emergence driven by prescribing pressure.
data.source: Carey et al. Lancet Microbe 2024 (PMC10914669)

data.definition: MIC-based; DCS = MIC 0.125–0.5 µg/mL; full resistance not detected
data.notes: Kinshasa 10-year surveillance. Period breakdown: 2007–2010: 14.0% DCS (27/193); 2011–2014: 36.3% DCS (61/168); 2015–2017: 25.0% DCS (27/108). No full resistance across entire decade. Rate for 2015–2017 period is 25%.
data.source: Makwela et al. PMC6405282 / Antimicrob Resist Infect Control 2019

data.notes: Tertiary referral hospital (Lusaka). Resistant: 5%; Intermediate: 30%. Authors noted the 30% intermediate rate as 'alarming'. Low absolute N; interpret with caution.
data.source: Mwine et al. PMC9216281 / J Glob Antimicrob Resist 2022

data.definition: CLSI; phenotypic disc diffusion (NICD national surveillance)
data.notes: NICD enteric fever surveillance: since 2015, ~16% of S. Typhi isolates tested not susceptible to ciprofloxacin (stable). In 2020, 12/69 (17%) not susceptible. 2003–2013 cumulative: 9.1% resistant (69/758). Cluster genomic study (2020–2021, N=38) showed 100% susceptibility—but these were clustered outbreak strains, not representative of all circulating isolates. The Global Typhoid Genomics Consortium noted >85% CipNS in South Africa based on genomic proxies, which may reflect imported/travel-associated lineages or referral bias.
data.source: NICD South Africa surveillance reports; Coetzee et al. Microb Genom 2023 (PMC10327498)

data.notes: A 2007–2012 rural Ghana study (bloodstream infections) found all S. Typhi susceptible to ciprofloxacin. A 2001–2009 surveillance study also found 100% susceptibility among S. Typhi. 2016–2018 study (N=68 S. Typhi) reported gyrA/gyrB QRDR mutations emerging (exact % not published in accessible full text). Ghana appears historically low-resistance but genomic surveillance suggests emerging DCS.
data.source: Marks et al. Clin Infect Dis 2016 (PMID 26933017); Oduro-Mensah et al. BMC Infect Dis 2019 (PMC)

data.definition: Disc diffusion; varied breakpoints across studies
data.notes: Data quality for Nigeria is poor — most typhoid diagnoses rely on Widal serology rather than blood culture. Small culture-confirmed studies: pre-2016 studies showed 100% susceptibility in Kano (malnourished children) and Lagos. 2016–2020 analysis (ESBL-producing isolates) showed 69% ciprofloxacin resistance, but this likely includes NTS and may represent resistant clones at tertiary referral centres. No nationally representative blood culture surveillance data retrievable for the 2015–2022 period.
data.source: Umoru et al. Afr Health Sci 2019; IOSR PHAR 2015 review

data.notes: No retrievable peer-reviewed blood culture–confirmed S. Typhi ciprofloxacin phenotypic surveillance data found for Uganda in 2015–2022. The multicountry sub-Saharan Africa molecular analysis (PMC4772832) did not identify Uganda-origin reduced-susceptibility isolates. TyphiNET lists Uganda as a country with limited genome data. Data gap.
data.source: Data gap — see multicountry African analysis: Kariuki et al. J Infect Dev Ctries 2015 (PMC4772832)

southAsia.description: Fluoroquinolone non-susceptibility is near-universal (85–98%) across Bangladesh, India, Nepal, and Pakistan for the 2016–2022 period. Pakistan additionally carries a high burden of XDR typhoid (ceftriaxone co-resistance, ~57% of isolates 2017–2019). India, Bangladesh, and Nepal show slightly lower XDR rates but ciprofloxacin monotherapy should be considered inactive.

southeastAsia.description: Cambodia and Vietnam have very high DCS (>90%), though full resistance (MIC ≥1) is rare. Laos has exceptionally low CipNS (~6% combined over 2000–2018). Indonesia and Philippines maintain low rates (<10%) but surveillance is sparse. The region has marked geographic heterogeneity.

subSaharanAfrica.description: Generally lower than South Asia. East Africa (Kenya ~43%, Tanzania/Zanzibar 69%) is higher than West Africa (Ghana ~0%) and Central Africa (DRC ~25%). Southern Africa is heterogeneous: Malawi rapidly emerging (2–33% in 2019), Zimbabwe escalating (4–22% over 2014–2017, then outbreak peaks of 75%), Zambia intermediate (35%), South Africa relatively stable (~16%). Nigeria lacks quality surveillance data.

keyReferences.dataType: Genomic proxy (QRDR mutations, accessible via typhiinet.net)
